output_text:
Tightened the prose, removed the "first work" overclaim and unsupported attribution, surfaced the missing numbers/baselines that the current draft hides, and flagged what no rewrite can fix.

## Revised text

We present **AskFirst**, a tabletop pick-and-place policy that defers execution when a natural-language instruction is referentially ambiguous with respect to the observed scene. AskFirst couples a VLM-based instruction parser with a lightweight abstention head that scores referential uncertainty over candidate targets; when the score falls below a calibrated threshold, the system emits a templated clarification request rather than grasping. Selective prediction is well studied in classification and, more recently, in LLM-based planning (e.g., KnowNo); our contribution is a self-contained, low-overhead realization for closed-loop manipulation and a controlled study of when deferral helps.

The abstention head is a two-layer MLP over fused VLM and scene features, trained on 12k synthetic (instruction, scene) pairs (50/50 ambiguous/unambiguous) produced by attribute-perturbation in cluttered renderings. The decision threshold is fit on a held-out validation split via Platt scaling; we report expected calibration error alongside accuracy. At inference, the policy either grasps or returns a two-option question over the top-2 candidates (e.g., *"did you mean the red mug or the red bowl?"*).

On a held-out set of 320 pairs (96 ambiguous by construction), AskFirst raises end-to-end success from 0.61 (always-execute baseline) to 0.78, with pick accuracy on the unambiguous subset within ±X of baseline (n.s.). Against an *always-ask* baseline (0.YY) and a VLM-confidence threshold without the learned head (0.ZZ), AskFirst trades Q clarification turns per episode for the gain. An ablation removing Platt calibration drops success to 0.WW, isolating the contribution of the calibrated threshold from the head itself.

The dominant residual failure mode is scenes with ≥3 plausible candidates, where the two-option template under-covers the referential set; Section 5 extends to k-option and dialogue-grounded clarification. Training takes ≈26 A100-hours; deployment runs at 8 Hz on the manipulator's onboard compute. We release the corpus, checkpoints, and the evaluation harness. Results are on synthetic scenes; a real-robot study on N=… trials is reported in Section 6 / left to future work (state which honestly).

## Recommendations the rewrite can't fix on its own

1. **Drop "first work" entirely.** Selective prediction in robot manipulation/planning has prior art (KnowNo, "Robots That Ask For Help," conformal-prediction-for-planning lines). Position against them explicitly or you will lose reviewers immediately.
2. **Report the unambiguous-subset accuracy as a number, not "unchanged."** A claim of no regression requires a CI or significance test.
3. **Add at minimum two baselines:** *always-ask* (upper bound on safety, lower bound on autonomy) and *VLM-logit threshold without the learned head* (isolates the value of the head).
4. **Report the cost of deferral**: clarifications-per-episode, false-defer rate on the unambiguous subset, and wall-clock added per episode. A success bump that comes from asking on every other trial is not the same contribution.
5. **The 0.61 → 0.78 gain is mechanically explained** by the construction: 30% of the test set is ambiguous, and an always-execute baseline must lose roughly half of those. Pre-empt this by decomposing the gain into ambiguous-subset recovery vs. unambiguous-subset preservation.
6. **Synthetic train *and* synthetic test is the central weakness.** Either add a small real-robot eval (even ~30 trials) or move the contribution framing from "materially improves manipulation" to "controlled study of selective deferral in a synthetic benchmark we release."
7. **"We attribute the gain to the system's willingness to defer"** is speculation without an ablation. Replace with the Platt-off / head-off ablations above.
8. **Soften "materially improves robotic manipulation performance under realistic instruction noise"** — synthetic perturbations are not realistic noise. Say "under the perturbation distribution we construct."
9. **The two-option template is a known limitation** stated in the conclusion but not in the contribution framing — move it up so reviewers don't feel they discovered it.
10. **Calibration claim needs a metric.** Report ECE pre/post Platt; otherwise "calibrated threshold" is just "tuned threshold."

## Uncertainty

Specific numbers in the revised text (±X, 0.YY, 0.ZZ, 0.WW, Q, N) are placeholders — I do not know your real ablation/baseline results. The KnowNo / "Robots That Ask For Help" prior-art pointers are from memory; verify exact citations before submission. The decomposition argument in recommendation 5 assumes the ambiguous baseline behaves near chance on those items; if your baseline has any disambiguation prior, the arithmetic shifts.
